allergens: fish (if fish sauce or salted shrimp used)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical side dish serving of kimchi is approximately 100 g, equivalent to roughly half a cup of fermented cabbage.

nutrient_source.title: Kimchi, fermented (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, NDB No. 11999 / FDC ID 169229)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for kimchi (fermented) used as primary nutrient source. Values represent traditional baechu-kimchi (napa cabbage kimchi). This is the highest-priority approved source available for this Korean fermented vegetable dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for kimchi was identified in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 15 is estimated based on the low-carbohydrate, high-fibre, fermented vegetable profile of kimchi. Fermented non-starchy vegetables consistently yield GI values in the 10–20 range in peer-reviewed literature. The estimate is conservative and consistent with values reported for analogous fermented vegetables in Atkinson et al. 2021 (AJCN). GL per 100 g serving = (15 × 2.4) / 100 = 0.36, rounded to 0.4.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~498 mg per 100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit kimchi intake to small portions (50–75 g) due to its high sodium content from the salting and fermentation process, and should account for it within their daily sodium budget of under 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index (~15) and low carbohydrate content (2.4 g per 100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Kimchi is well-suited for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its very low GI of approximately 15 and minimal carbohydrate load; emerging evidence also suggests that the probiotic content from fermentation may support glycaemic control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Dual profile: beneficial bioactive compounds offset by high sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Kimchi provides beneficial bioactive compounds including antioxidants, dietary fibre, and fermentation-derived probiotics that may support cardiovascular health, but its high sodium content (~498 mg per 100 g) warrants portion control for individuals with cardiovascular disease or those managing blood pressure.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Kimchi (김치) is a traditional Korean fermented vegetable dish, most commonly made from napa cabbage (baechu) salted and seasoned with a paste of Korean red pepper flakes (gochugaru), garlic, ginger, green onions, and typically fish sauce or salted shrimp. The cabbage is first salted to draw out moisture, then coated with the seasoning paste and left to ferment at ambient or cool temperatures for days to weeks. The result is a tangy, spicy, umami-rich condiment with a crunchy to soft texture depending on fermentation duration. Kimchi is a cornerstone of Korean cuisine, served as a banchan (side dish) at virtually every Korean meal, and is also used as an ingredient in dishes such as kimchi jjigae (stew), kimchi fried rice, and kimchi pancakes. Nutritionally, kimchi is very low in calories (~15 kcal per 100 g) and carbohydrates (~2.4 g per 100 g), making it suitable for low-carbohydrate and diabetic diets. It is a meaningful source of dietary fibre (1.6 g per 100 g), vitamin C (18 mg), vitamin A (26 mcg RAE), and folate (28 mcg DFE). Its primary clinical concern is its high sodium content (~498 mg per 100 g), which requires portion awareness for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The fermentation process generates lactic acid bacteria with probiotic properties, which may support gut health and glycaemic regulation. In Singapore, kimchi is widely available as a side dish at Korean restaurants and Korean-influenced food stalls.

How much carbohydrate is in kimchi?
What is the GI of kimchi?
meal_context: Accompaniment to rice and main dishes
Ingredient in kimchi bokkeumbap (kimchi fried rice)

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 169229), which is an approved secondary global source and provides a well-documented entry for fermented kimchi. No HPB Singapore Foodo or FAO/INFOODS SEA entry was identified for kimchi specifically. GI is an analogous estimate based on fermented non-starchy vegetable profiles from peer-reviewed literature, not a direct measurement. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: Serving size set to 100 g for per-100 g nutrient alignment; the field survey listing is for a 300 g portion which would represent approximately 3 standard servings. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central source for this fermented vegetable product. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central; kimchi is a plant-based fermented food with no cholesterol. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed zero as kimchi is a plant-based fermented food; trace B12 from fermentation is below detection threshold and not reported in USDA source. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central; kimchi contains no meaningful vitamin D. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for kimchi was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GL per serving = (15 × 2.4) / 100 = 0.36, rounded to 0.4. Ontology: FoodOn URI FOODON_03311171 and FoodEx2 code A00LC are used as best available matches for kimchi; SNOMED CT code 227493005 is used as exact match. Dietary flags: is_vegetarian and is_vegan set to false as traditional kimchi typically contains fish sauce or salted shrimp; vegan versions exist but are not the default. is_halal set to false as traditional kimchi contains non-halal seafood ingredients. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g values sourced from USDA FDC. potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g all sourced from USDA FDC SR Legacy FDC ID 169229.
